mnemonics.title: EC2 S3 RDS Lambda - The AWS Family
mnemonics.content: EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is like a rented computer, S3 (Simple Storage Service) is like a storage room, RDS (Relational Database Service) is like a managed database manager, and Lambda is like hiring someone to do tasks only when needed - they all work together like different family members in a tech household.

mnemonics.title: Regions and AZs - The Global Village
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'Regions Represent Earth's Geographic Areas, AZs Are Isolated Data Centers' - R-R-E-G-A, A-A-I-D-C. Think of Regions as countries and Availability Zones (AZs) as buildings in that country - each building is separate but connected.

mnemonics.title: IAM - Identity and Access Management
mnemonics.content: IAM = 'Identify Who Can Access What' - It manages WHO (Users/Groups/Roles) can do WHAT (Permissions) on WHICH resources. Remember: 'I Am Mortal' - because proper access control is a matter of life and death for security!

mnemonics.title: VPC - Virtual Private Cloud
mnemonics.content: VPC is your own private island in the AWS ocean - completely isolated, you control who enters and leaves. Think: 'Your Very Private Castle' - with firewalls (Security Groups) as guards.

mnemonics.title: AWS Pricing Pillars
mnemonics.content: Pay for what you use: Compute (CPU time), Storage (GB stored), Data Transfer (outbound traffic). Remember: 'C-S-D' = Compute, Storage, Data - like a COD bill where you Pay On Delivery!

mnemonics.title: Serverless with Lambda
mnemonics.content: Lambda = 'Little A-mazing Machine Auto-running' - you write code, AWS manages servers. No server to provision, no OS to patch. Think: 'LAMBDA - Lights And Machines By Deployment Auto' - it just runs when triggered!

acronyms.usage: Use when you need virtual servers (like a computer in the cloud) to run applications. You control the OS and software.
Use for storing files, images, videos, backups, and any object-based data. Think of it as an infinitely large, highly durable filing cabinet.
Use for managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server). AWS handles backups, patches, and replication.
Use to create users, groups, roles, and define permissions. Essential for security - always use IAM, never use root account for daily tasks.
Use to create your own virtual network in AWS. You define IP ranges, subnets, routing tables, and security groups.
Use to distribute incoming traffic across multiple EC2 instances or services. Ensures no single server gets overloaded.
Use for persistent block storage volumes that attach to EC2 instances. Like a hard drive for your virtual computer.
Use for sending notifications via email, SMS, or HTTP endpoints. Pub-sub messaging service for alerting.
Use for message queuing between application components. Decouples systems - sender puts message in queue, receiver processes later.
Use for running code without provisioning servers. Pay only for compute time used. Triggered by events (file upload, API call, etc).
Use for content delivery network - caches content at edge locations globally for faster access to users worldwide.
Use for fast, scalable NoSQL database. Fully managed, serverless option available. Good for high-traffic applications.
Use for monitoring AWS resources and applications. Tracks metrics, logs, and sets alarms. Your surveillance system in the cloud.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a globe with boxes (Regions) scattered around. Each box contains smaller isolated rooms (Availability Zones). Think of Regions as countries and AZs as earthquake-proof buildings in that country - if one building falls, others still stand.

Imagine VPC as your own gated community. The Main Route Table is the main road, Subnets are different sections (public where guests enter, private for residents only), and Security Groups are guards checking IDs at each house.

Think of a restaurant host distributing guests across all available tables. ELB is that host - ensuring no table is overwhelmed while maximizing seating capacity. Guests (traffic) don't know which table they'll get!
Picture a concert venue that expands seats during popular events and shrinks during quiet times. Auto Scaling Group adds/removes EC2 instances based on demand - like elastic seating!

Imagine an infinitely large, organized library with perfect librarians. You give an address (key) to retrieve any book (object). The library never closes, never loses a book (99.999999999% durability), and you only pay for shelf space used.

Think of a restaurant where you only pay for the specific dishes cooked, not for renting the kitchen or paying chefs when they're idle. Lambda is like ordering à la carte computing - just the function execution, nothing more.

Picture AWS as the builder of a house (infrastructure - physical security, hardware) and you as the owner (software, data, access management). AWS maintains the foundation and walls; you lock doors, install alarms, and keep valuables safe.

analogies.analogy: EC2 is like owning a car - you maintain it, pay for parking (idle time), fill the tank (compute). Lambda is like using Uber - you only pay when the car takes you somewhere, no maintenance worries.

S3 is like a shared document storage room in an office - anyone with permission can drop or retrieve files from anywhere. EBS is like your personal hard drive attached to your specific computer - only that computer can use it directly.

RDS is like a structured filing cabinet with labeled folders (tables with schemas, SQL queries). DynamoDB is like a NoSQL document organizer - flexible, fast for key-value lookups, scales automatically like a growing company's cubicles.

Public subnet is like a storefront (visible to everyone, customers enter). Private subnet is like the back office (only employees access, stores sensitive data). Both exist in the same building but separated by security.

Security Groups are like bouncers at a club - they check a list of allowed guests (rules), verify IDs (ports/protocols), and can kick out troublemakers. They're stateful - if you enter, you're automatically allowed out.

CloudFront is like having branches of a library in every neighborhood. Instead of traveling to the main library (origin server), you get books from the nearest branch (edge location) - much faster for readers!

Like electricity bill - you pay for what you consume. Leave lights on (run services), pay more. Turn off (stop instances), no charge. Some services like S3 have small ongoing costs for just storing things, even unused.

SQS is like a restaurant's order queue. Kitchen (processing service) might be busy, so orders (messages) pile up in the queue. The kitchen pulls orders when ready - nothing gets lost, and the customer (sender) doesn't wait for cooking to complete.
